past_medical_history: Migraine with aura, diagnosed in her 20s, now infrequent (2-3 episodes per year), managed with sumatriptan PRN
Hypothyroidism, diagnosed 6 years ago, well-controlled on levothyroxine
Generalized anxiety disorder, diagnosed 3 years ago, managed with buspirone
family_history: Father had 'behavioral changes' and dementia beginning at age 70, was placed in memory care at 74, died at 78 — never formally diagnosed or genetically tested
Mother alive at 82, had breast cancer treated at 65, no neurological disease
One sister, age 61, has anxiety and depression
Paternal uncle reportedly had personality changes and dementia in his 60s

social_history.occupation: Bank branch manager for 22 years; currently on medical leave for 4 months after multiple workplace complaints about judgment and behavior
social_history.living_situation: Lives with her husband in a two-story home. One adult daughter lives nearby.
social_history.alcohol: Social drinker, 2-3 glasses of wine per week. Husband notes no increase in alcohol consumption.
social_history.exercise: Previously active — ran 5K races and attended spin classes 3 times weekly. Has stopped exercising over the past 6 months due to apathy.

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person and place. Oriented to month and year but not exact date (1 day off). Mildly disinhibited during exam — makes inappropriate jokes about the examiner's appearance and laughs at her own errors. Utilization behavior observed: picks up the examiner's pen and starts writing on the desk without being asked. MMSE 22/30 — lost 3 points on recall (0/3 words at 5 minutes, retrieved 1 with category cue), lost 1 point on orientation (date), lost 2 points on serial sevens (3 errors), lost 2 points on sentence writing and figure copy (wrote a fragmented sentence, figure copy poorly organized). MoCA 18/30 with deficits in delayed recall (0/5, retrieved 2 with cues), trail-making (0/1), verbal fluency (5 F-words in 60 seconds), clock drawing (2/3 — numbers correct but hand placement imprecise), and abstraction (0/2). Verbal fluency: 8 animals in 60 seconds, 5 F-words in 60 seconds. Applause sign positive — unable to stop clapping at 3 (clapped 7 times). Motor impersistence on tongue protrusion (could not maintain protrusion for 20 seconds).
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. Visual fields full to confrontation. Facial expression mildly flattened. Hearing intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Normal bulk and tone throughout. Strength 5/5 in all extremities. No pronator drift. No tremor. No myoclonus. Mild grasp reflexes present bilaterally.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch and pinprick intact throughout. Vibration sense intact at great toes bilaterally. Proprioception intact. No cortical sensory deficits.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric at biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellar, and Achilles bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally. Palmomental reflex present bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Heel-to-shin testing normal. Rapid alternating movements intact.
neurological_exam.gait: Steady gait with normal base and stride length. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative. No gait abnormalities. No postural instability.
neurological_exam.additional: No paratonic rigidity. Mild bilateral grasp reflexes present. No alien limb phenomenon. Palmomental reflex present bilaterally. Go/no-go task: 4 errors in 10 trials (impaired inhibitory control). Luria hand sequence: could not learn the 3-step sequence after 5 demonstrations (severely impaired).

patient.chief_complaint: Personality changes and poor judgment at work
patient.history_present_illness: Ms. Sofia Reyes is a 58-year-old right-handed bank branch manager who is referred to the neurology outpatient clinic by her primary care physician for evaluation of progressive behavioral changes and cognitive decline over the past 18 months. Her husband, who accompanies her, provides the majority of the history as the patient tends to minimize and joke about her difficulties. Approximately 18 months ago, her husband first noticed subtle changes in her personality. She began making inappropriate comments to colleagues and customers at the bank, including remarking on a customer's weight and making off-color jokes during staff meetings — behavior completely out of character for someone who had been known for her professionalism and interpersonal skills. Over the following months, she became increasingly apathetic, losing interest in activities she previously enjoyed including running, cooking, and socializing with friends. She has had progressive difficulty with organizational tasks at work — she was previously meticulous with documentation and compliance protocols, but began making errors in loan approvals, forgot to file regulatory reports, and could not manage multiple tasks simultaneously. Her judgment deteriorated — she made several impulsive online purchases totaling over $8,000 in a single month, bought items she already owned, and signed up for multiple streaming subscriptions she never used. After receiving formal complaints from three customers and two staff members about her inappropriate remarks, she was placed on medical leave 4 months ago. She was seen by a psychiatrist who diagnosed depression and tried sertraline 100 mg daily for 8 weeks, then switched to venlafaxine 150 mg for 6 weeks — neither improved her behavioral symptoms, and the language difficulties continued to progress. Importantly, her husband reports that in addition to the behavioral changes, she has developed concurrent memory problems. She frequently forgets conversations from earlier in the day, repeats questions multiple times, forgets appointments despite using calendar reminders, and recently forgot that her daughter had visited the previous weekend. She also has difficulty learning new information — she could not figure out how to use a new coffee maker despite repeated instruction. She has not gotten lost but has had difficulty planning meals and following recipes she previously made from memory. She denies visual hallucinations, paranoid ideation, or delusional thinking. Sleep is unremarkable. There is no history of head trauma, seizures, or substance abuse. Her father's history of behavioral dementia beginning at age 70 is noted.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 8 Hz alpha activity over occipital regions, at the lower limit of normal
background.anterior_posterior_gradient: Mildly disrupted with increased frontal theta activity

findings.description: Intermittent bifrontal theta slowing (5-7 Hz) present throughout the recording, more prominent during drowsiness

eeg.impression: Mildly abnormal EEG showing intermittent bifrontal theta slowing with frontal predominance. No epileptiform discharges. No electrographic seizures. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Routine 30-minute recording in the awake and drowsy states.

findings.location: Bilateral frontal lobes, left greater than right, including orbitofrontal cortex and dorsolateral prefrontal cortex

signal_characteristics.T1: Bilateral frontal cortical atrophy with widened sulci, most prominent in orbitofrontal and dorsolateral prefrontal regions. Left hemisphere more affected than right.
signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: Mild periventricular white matter hyperintensities, Fazekas grade 1

signal_characteristics.T1: Mild bilateral anterior temporal atrophy, left greater than right
Mild bilateral hippocampal atrophy, Scheltens medial temporal atrophy score 2 bilaterally

findings.location: Posterior parietal and occipital cortex, basal ganglia, brainstem, cerebellum

signal_characteristics.T1: No focal atrophy in posterior cortical regions

volumetrics.frontal_volume: Reduced bilaterally, left greater than right, below 10th percentile for age

additional_observations: No acute infarcts on DWI sequences
No mass lesions or pathological enhancement post-gadolinium
No ventriculomegaly out of proportion to sulcal atrophy
The frontal-predominant atrophy pattern is notable
mri.impression: Frontal-predominant cortical atrophy (bilateral, left greater than right), involving both orbitofrontal and dorsolateral prefrontal cortex, with mild anterior temporal atrophy. Mild hippocampal atrophy (Scheltens 2) is also present. The frontal-predominant pattern raises a differential that includes behavioral variant frontotemporal dementia (bvFTD) and frontal variant Alzheimer disease. The presence of hippocampal atrophy is somewhat atypical for pure bvFTD and may favor AD or mixed pathology. Mild age-appropriate periventricular white matter changes (Fazekas 1). Clinical correlation recommended.

csf.interpretation: Opening pressure: 13 cmH2O. WBC: 1 cells/uL (N/A PMN/N/A lymph). Protein: 38 mg/dL. Glucose: 62 mg/dL (ratio 0.67 (serum glucose 92 mg/dL)). Amyloid_beta_42: 410 pg/mL (reference >600 pg/mL) - REDUCED. Phosphorylated_tau_181: 62 pg/mL (reference <24 pg/mL) - SIGNIFICANTLY ELEVATED. Total_tau: 580 pg/mL (reference <400 pg/mL) - ELEVATED.

findings.test: Wisconsin Card Sorting Test - Categories Completed

output.impression: Abnormal values: Trail Making Test Part A 58 seconds (H), Trail Making Test Part B Discontinued at 300 seconds seconds (H), Wisconsin Card Sorting Test - Categories Completed 1 categories (L), Stroop Color-Word Interference -2.4 z-score (H), Go/No-Go Task 40% commission errors % (H), Verbal Fluency - Phonemic (FAS) 14 total words (L), Verbal Fluency - Semantic (Animals) 9 words/minute (L), CVLT-3 Long Delay Free Recall 3/16 words (H), Clock Drawing 7/10 points (H), Frontal Behavioral Inventory - Informant Rating 32 points (H).

findings.region: Diffuse cortical amyloid deposition with frontal predominance
findings.signal: Florbetapir_PET: Elevated cortical-to-cerebellar SUVr of 1.52 (positive threshold >1.11); Distribution: Diffuse bilateral cortical amyloid binding with highest uptake in frontal cortex, followed by parietal and temporal cortex. Posterior cingulate and precuneus also elevated.

output.impression: STRONGLY POSITIVE amyloid PET scan. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings.region: Bifrontal and anterior temporal cortex, with additional posterior temporal-parietal hypometabolism
findings.signal: FDG_PET: Reduced FDG uptake in bilateral frontal lobes (orbitofrontal > dorsolateral prefrontal), anterior temporal cortex, AND posterior temporoparietal cortex; Pattern: Bifrontal hypometabolism predominates, but posterior temporoparietal hypometabolism is also present — the posterior involvement is unusual for pure bvFTD and suggests AD pathology

output.impression: FDG-PET demonstrates bifrontal and anterior temporal hypometabolism with additional posterior temporoparietal and posterior cingulate/precuneus hypometabolism. Clinical correlation recommended.

abnormal_values_summary: APOE E3/E4 — heterozygous for e4 risk allele

differential.key_distinguishing: Positive AD biomarkers (reduced amyloid, elevated tau) and concurrent episodic memory impairment favor AD over FTD; absence of FTD-gene mutations (MAPT, GRN, C9orf72) argues against genetic FTD
Behavioral and executive symptoms predominate over memory — unusual presentation for typical amnestic AD
Progressive course over 18 months, structural brain atrophy on MRI, and positive AD biomarkers exclude primary psychiatric etiology
No gait disturbance, no urinary incontinence, no ventriculomegaly out of proportion to cortical atrophy on MRI

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed behavioral history from patient and informant, assessing for disinhibition, apathy, loss of empathy, and compulsive behaviors
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Progressive disinhibition, apathy, poor judgment, and personality change, plus concurrent memory deficits

optimal_actions.action: Perform comprehensive neurological examination including frontal lobe signs (grasp reflex, utilization behavior, applause sign, go/no-go, Luria sequences)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Frontal release signs, disinhibition, impaired executive tasks, with concurrent memory impairment on screening

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with volumetric protocol
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Frontal-predominant atrophy with mild hippocampal atrophy — pattern raises both bvFTD and frontal AD in the differential

optimal_actions.action: Order dementia screening labs: CBC, BMP, TSH, vitamin B12, folate
Order comprehensive neuropsych battery assessing both executive AND memory domains
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Severe executive dysfunction with concurrent episodic memory impairment — the combined deficit profile favors AD over pure bvFTD

optimal_actions.action: Obtain AD biomarkers (CSF amyloid/tau or amyloid PET) to differentiate AD from FTD
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Positive AD biomarkers confirming Alzheimer pathology

optimal_actions.action: Consider FTD-gene panel to exclude genetic FTD given family history
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Negative MAPT/GRN/C9orf72 — ruling out genetic FTD

optimal_actions.action: Refer for genetic counseling given early-onset presentation and family history

critical_actions: Obtain AD biomarkers (CSF or amyloid PET) to differentiate AD from FTD
Order comprehensive neuropsych battery assessing both executive AND memory domains
Consider FTD-gene panel to exclude genetic FTD
Assess for concurrent memory impairment which favors AD over pure bvFTD
Refer for genetic counseling given early-onset presentation
contraindicated_actions: Diagnosing bvFTD without AD biomarker testing
Treating behavioral symptoms with antipsychotics as first line without proper diagnosis
Dismissing cognitive symptoms as purely psychiatric
key_reasoning_points: Behavioral/executive symptoms as the predominant presentation mimics bvFTD
Concurrent episodic memory impairment is unusual for bvFTD and suggests AD
Strongly positive AD biomarkers (low amyloid, high tau) confirm AD as the underlying pathology
Negative FTD gene panel argues against genetic FTD
FDG-PET showing both frontal AND posterior hypometabolism supports AD over FTD (FTD typically spares posterior regions)
Early-onset presentation (age 58) raises the pre-test probability of atypical AD variants
Father's undiagnosed behavioral dementia could represent either familial FTD or familial AD

red_herrings.correct_interpretation: Frontal variant AD can produce identical imaging pattern; biomarkers are required to differentiate

red_herrings.data_point: Father with behavioral changes and dementia
red_herrings.correct_interpretation: Father was never genetically tested; could equally represent familial AD with frontal presentation
These frontal signs can occur in frontal variant AD as well

metadata.diagnostic_challenge: Differentiating frontal variant AD from behavioral variant FTD in a patient with frontal-predominant behavioral and executive deficits, complicated by a family history suggestive of FTD
metadata.educational_value: Teaches recognition of frontal variant AD as an atypical AD presentation that mimics bvFTD, and the critical role of AD biomarkers in differentiating these conditions
key_educational_points: Frontal variant AD can be clinically indistinguishable from bvFTD based on behavioral symptoms alone
Concurrent episodic memory impairment in a behavioral/dysexecutive presentation should raise suspicion for AD rather than pure FTD
AD biomarkers (amyloid PET or CSF) are essential for differentiating frontal AD from bvFTD
FDG-PET showing posterior temporoparietal involvement in addition to frontal hypometabolism favors AD over FTD
Family history of behavioral dementia does not confirm FTD — familial AD can present with frontal symptoms
Negative FTD gene panel (MAPT, GRN, C9orf72) helps exclude genetic FTD but does not rule out sporadic bvFTD
